1: יוֹם תְּרוּעָה (yom teru'ah, H8643): 'a day of trumpet blasting / a day of loud shouting.' The first day of the seventh month (Tishri 1) is the yom teru'ah — the day of the teru'ah blast. Teru'ah (H8643) is the staccato, alarm-style blast of the trumpet (distinct from the sustained teqi'ah): it signals urgent gathering, divine approach, and battle alarm (Numbers 10:5-6). The seventh month is the most liturgically dense of the year: three major holy occasions fall within it (Numbers 29:1, 7, 12). The month begins with noise — the urgent, summoning call of the trumpet.
2: וַעֲשִׂיתֶם עֹלָה לְרֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ לַיהוָה (va'asitem olah lere'ach nichoach la-YHWH): 'you shall make a burnt offering as a pleasing aroma to YHWH.' The Rosh Hashanah (Day of Trumpets) offering is notably smaller than the New Moon offering: one bull, one ram, seven lambs (vs. New Moon's two bulls, one ram, seven lambs). Yet it falls in the same miqra-qodesh (holy assembly) category. The specific shape of the offering is always determined by YHWH's command, not human calculation about what seems appropriate for the occasion's importance.
3: מִנְחָתָם (minchatam): 'their grain offering.' The Day of Trumpets grain offering proportions: three-tenths per bull, two-tenths per ram — consistent with the other festival offerings. The repetition of familiar proportions gives the worshipper a stable, learnable system. The Sinai legislation is pedagogically structured: the same proportional grammar recurs throughout, forming liturgical habit through repetition.
4: וְעִשָּׂרוֹן אֶחָד לַכֶּבֶשׂ הָאֶחָד (v'issaron echad lakeves ha'echad): 'one-tenth for each lamb.' One-tenth of an ephah of fine flour for each of the seven lambs of the Rosh Hashanah offering. The uniformity across festivals in the lamb grain offering (always one-tenth each) reflects a systematic logic: what is owed per lamb is fixed regardless of which festival it serves. Consistency in measure is a form of covenantal faithfulness.
5: לְכַפֵּר עֲלֵיכֶם (lekapper aleikhem, H3722): 'to make atonement for you.' As with every festival (cf. Numbers 28:15, 22, 30), the sin offering goat at Rosh Hashanah effects corporate atonement. The seventh month's opening day begins with both the summoning teru'ah and the community's corporate expiation — the month that climaxes in Yom Kippur begins with a foretaste of atonement.
6: מִלְּבַד עֹלַת הַחֹדֶשׁ וּמִנְחָתָהּ (milevad olat hachodesh uminchatah): 'in addition to the monthly burnt offering and its grain offering.' The layering principle: Rosh Hashanah falls on the first of the month, so both the New Moon offering (Numbers 28:11-15) and the Rosh Hashanah offering are required simultaneously — stacked on the perpetual tamid. The day of the New Moon that opens the seventh month is the most heavily layered offering day outside Yom Kippur and Sukkot: daily + monthly + Rosh Hashanah offerings all brought together.
7: בֶּעָשׂוֹר לַחֹדֶשׁ הַשְּׁבִיעִי הַזֶּה (be'asor lachodesh hashvi'i hazeh): 'on the tenth of this seventh month.' Yom Kippur — the Day of Atonement. וְעִנִּיתֶם אֶת-נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם (v'initem et-nafshoteikhem, H6031+H5315): 'you shall afflict your souls.' Anah (H6031, to afflict, humble, oppress) applied reflexively: Israel afflicts/humbles herself. Nefesh (H5315, soul, life, self) — the affliction is of the whole inner person, traditionally expressed through fasting. מְלָאכָה לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ (melakhah lo ta'asu): the prohibition here is stronger — melakhah without the qualifier avodah of the other festivals — suggesting total rest.
8: וְהִקְרַבְתֶּם עֹלָה לַיהוָה (vehiqravtem olah la-YHWH): 'you shall present a burnt offering to YHWH.' The Yom Kippur communal offering: one bull, one ram, seven lambs (same as Rosh Hashanah). This is the public festival sacrifice; the unique Yom Kippur ritual of Lev 16 — with the high priest's two goats, the scapegoat, and the entry into the Holy of Holies — is not repeated here but referenced in Numbers 29:11. These offerings are in addition to Lev 16's special rites, not a replacement for them.
9: מִנְחָתָם (minchatam): 'their grain offering.' Yom Kippur grain offering proportions are identical to Rosh Hashanah (three-tenths/bull, two-tenths/ram). The consistency reinforces the system's memorability and signals that no one festival's animals are intrinsically more valuable than another's: the atonement's efficacy rests not in the scale of the offering but in YHWH's acceptance of it.
10: עִשָּׂרוֹן לַכֶּבֶשׂ הָאֶחָד (issaron lakeves ha'echad): 'one-tenth for each of the seven lambs.' Standard lamb grain offering. The formulaic precision of these verses serves catechetical as well as liturgical purposes: the text trains Israel to associate specific measures with specific occasions, forming liturgical memory across generations.
11: מִלְּבַד חַטַּאת הַכִּפֻּרִים (milevad chatta't hakippurim, H3725): 'in addition to the sin offering of the atonements.' The crucial phrase: the communal burnt and sin offerings of Numbers 29:8-10 are additional to the chatta't hakippurim — the special Yom Kippur atonement ritual of Lev 16. The term kippurim (H3725) — a plural of intensity — appears only in the compound Yom Kippurim (Day of Atonements). The greatest annual atonement event combines the regular festival sacrifice system with the unrepeatable, once-per-year high-priestly entry into the Holy of Holies.
12: בַּחֲמִשָּׁה עָשָׂר יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ הַשְּׁבִיעִי (bachamisha asar yom lachodesh hashvi'i): 'on the 15th of the seventh month.' Sukkot (Festival of Tabernacles/Booths) begins five days after Yom Kippur. Chag la-YHWH — 'a feast to YHWH.' Sukkot is the most joyful of the three chagim — the harvest festival (Deuteronomy 16:13-15 commands: 'you shall rejoice in your feast'). Shivat yamim — seven days — the longest of the festivals, reflecting the autumn harvest's significance as the culmination of Israel's agricultural year.
13: שְׁלֹשָׁה עָשָׂר פָּרִים (sheloshah asar parim, H6499): 'thirteen young bulls.' Day 1 of Sukkot begins with thirteen bulls — the largest single-day animal offering in the entire liturgical calendar. Two rams and fourteen yearling lambs complete the burnt offering. The remarkable scale signals Sukkot's status as the premier harvest festival, celebrating YHWH's provision through the agricultural year. The rabbinic tradition associates the total 70 bulls offered across the seven days of Sukkot with the 70 nations of Gen 10 — Israel interceding for the nations at its greatest feast.
14: שְׁלֹשָׁה עֶשְׂרֹנִים לַפָּר הָאֶחָד (sheloshah esronim lapar ha'echad): 'three-tenths for each bull.' Thirteen bulls means thirteen grain offerings of three-tenths each — 39 tenths of flour for the bulls alone on Sukkot Day 1. The scale of the grain offering matches the scale of the animal offering. Bishetai esronim la'ayil ha'echad — two-tenths for each of the two rams. The mathematical precision is sustained even when the numbers become very large.
15: עִשָּׂרוֹן עִשָּׂרוֹן לַכֶּבֶשׂ הָאֶחָד (issaron issaron lakeves ha'echad): 'one-tenth for each lamb.' Fourteen lambs × one-tenth = 14 tenths. The total grain offering on Sukkot Day 1 is 39 (bulls) + 4 (rams) + 14 (lambs) = 57 tenths of an ephah. The sheer volume underscores the festival's grandeur. Every proportion is specified; nothing is left to approximation or improvisation.
16: וּשְׂעִיר-עִזִּים אֶחָד חַטָּאת (use'ir-izzim echad chatta't): 'one male goat as a sin offering.' Even on Sukkot Day 1 — the grandest day of offering in the calendar — the sin offering goat is present. The juxtaposition of massive celebration (13 bulls) and the solitary sin offering goat expresses the two movements of Israel's worship: exuberant gratitude for provision and clear-eyed acknowledgment of sin. Both belong; neither can be omitted.
17: וּבַיּוֹם הַשֵּׁנִי פָּרִים בְּנֵי-בָקָר שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר (uvayom hasheni parim b'nei-vaqar shenim asar): 'on the second day: twelve young bulls.' Sukkot Day 2 reduces by one bull (13 → 12). The descending sequence — 13, 12, 11, 10, 9, 8, 7 across the seven days — is unique in the sacrificial legislation; no other festival feature operates with a diminishing count. The rams (always two) and lambs (always fourteen) remain constant; only the bulls decrease. The pattern is given without explanation in the text itself.
18: וּמִנְחָתָם וְנִסְכֵּיהֶם (uminchatam veniskeihem): 'their grain offerings and drink offerings.' The formula kamishpatam (H4941) — 'according to their ordinance/proper measure' — covers the grain and drink offerings for all the animals on Day 2. Once the proportional system is established (three-tenths/bull, two-tenths/ram, one-tenth/lamb), the text economizes: kamishpatam references the established proportions rather than repeating them in full for every day.
19: וּשְׂעִיר-עִזִּים אֶחָד חַטָּאת (use'ir-izzim echad chatta't): 'one male goat as a sin offering.' Day 2's sin offering is identical to Day 1's. The constancy of the sin offering across all seven days — one goat, unchanged — while the bull count descends communicates a structural truth: the need for atonement does not decrease as the festival progresses. The celebration's joy increases in some sense (Israel moves deeper into the festival), but the acknowledgment of sin is no less necessary on Day 7 than on Day 1.
20: וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי פָּרִים אַחַד עָשָׂר (uvayom hashlishi parim achad asar): 'on the third day: eleven bulls.' The descending sequence continues. Day 3 brings 11 bulls — still more than any other festival (New Moon: 2; Passover week daily: 2; Shavuot: 2) — demonstrating that even the lowest point of Sukkot's bull offering exceeds the high points of other festivals. Sukkot's scale is categorically different from all other occasions in the calendar.
21: וּמִנְחָתָם וְנִסְכֵּיהֶם (uminchatam veniskeihem): 'their grain offerings and drink offerings.' Kamishpatam again — according to their ordinance. The economy of the formula is the economy of mastery: the system is now so well-established that the full prescription can be abbreviated. Each day's offering set is uniquely specified (bull count) yet governed by the same proportional grammar (grain and drink offering amounts).
22: וּשְׂעִיר אֶחָד חַטָּאת (use'ir echad chatta't): 'one goat as a sin offering.' Day 3 sin offering — unchanged. The goat holds constant through all seven days of Sukkot, through all the festival's variation. The sin offering is not diminished by repetition; its constancy is itself a theological statement about the permanent human condition requiring expiation.
23: וּבַיּוֹם הָרְבִיעִי פָּרִים עֲשָׂרָה (uvayom harvi'i parim asarah): 'on the fourth day: ten bulls.' The midpoint of Sukkot. Ten is both the midpoint of the 13-7 sequence and a number with strong covenantal resonance in Israel: the ten words (commandments), ten plagues, ten spies. The fourth day reaches a round number — ten — that may function as the structural center of the week-long offering sequence.
24: מִנְחָתָם וְנִסְכֵּיהֶם (minchatam veniskeihem): 'their grain offerings and drink offerings.' Kamishpatam — according to their ordinance. The structural economy of the repeated formula reinforces that the proportions are fixed law, not approximation. Israel does not negotiate the offering amounts; she executes the ordained measure precisely.
25: וּשְׂעִיר-עִזִּים אֶחָד חַטָּאת (use'ir-izzim echad chatta't): 'one male goat as a sin offering.' Day 4 sin offering — invariant. The repetition of the identical sin offering against the backdrop of the declining bull sequence highlights the constancy of Israel's sinfulness relative to the variability of celebration. Festival intensity may fluctuate; the need for atonement does not.
26: וּבַיּוֹם הַחֲמִישִׁי פָּרִים תִּשְׁעָה (uvayom hachamishi parim tish'ah): 'on the fifth day: nine bulls.' Nine bulls — the sequence descends from the high of thirteen toward the conclusion of seven. Sheney eilim and fourteen lambs remain constant, as they do throughout the week. The two rams may evoke covenant confirmation (covenant sacrifices often involved pairs: Gen 15; Exod 24) — their constancy through the week marks the unchanging covenantal ground of the festival.
27: וּמִנְחָתָם וְנִסְכֵּיהֶם (uminchatam veniskeihem): 'their grain offerings and drink offerings.' Kamishpatam. By Day 5 of Sukkot, the worshipper has internalized the proportional system through four repetitions. The text's use of the abbreviated formula is a pedagogical feature: repetition with economy trains the worshipper in the grammar of sacrifice.
28: וּשְׂעִיר אֶחָד חַטָּאת (use'ir echad chatta't): 'one goat as a sin offering.' Day 5 — constant. The sin offering's constancy throughout Sukkot's seven days may be read as a structural comment on the festival itself: Sukkot celebrates harvest abundance and divine provision, but abundance does not reduce the human need for atonement. The greatest feast of YHWH's provision is bracketed and pierced by the acknowledgment of human sin.
29: וּבַיּוֹם הַשִּׁשִּׁי פָּרִים שְׁמֹנָה (uvayom hashishi parim shmonah): 'on the sixth day: eight bulls.' The penultimate day. Eight bulls — one more than the final day's seven. The sequence nears its completion: 13→12→11→10→9→8→7. Adding the bulls across all seven days gives 13+12+11+10+9+8+7 = 70. The number 70 resonates throughout Israel's story: 70 nations (Gen 10), 70 elders of Israel (Exodus 24:9), 70 years of exile (Jeremiah 25:11), 70 members of Jacob's family in Egypt (Genesis 46:27). Rabbinic tradition connects the 70 Sukkot bulls to intercession for the 70 nations.
30: מִנְחָתָם וְנִסְכֵּיהֶם (minchatam veniskeihem): 'their grain offerings and drink offerings.' Kamishpatam. The penultimate day follows the same proportional grammar as all the others. The system's consistency through six repetitions has fully formed the liturgical pattern in the reader's mind. Day 6's offerings are simply the prescribed measures applied to eight bulls, two rams, fourteen lambs.
31: וּשְׂעִיר אֶחָד חַטָּאת (use'ir echad chatta't): 'one goat as a sin offering.' Day 6 — unchanged. The sixth day sin offering recalls the sixth day of creation (Genesis 1:31), when God looked at all he had made and saw it was very good — yet even that good creation required, after the fall, the ongoing mechanism of atonement. Sukkot celebrates the fruit of a good creation, and the sin offering acknowledges that the creation's goodness does not eliminate the creature's sinfulness.
32: וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי פָּרִים שִׁבְעָה (uvayom hashvi'i parim shiv'ah): 'on the seventh day: seven bulls.' The sequence completes: seven bulls on the seventh day. Seven is Israel's number of completion and covenant (seven-day week, seventh-year sabbatical, seventh-month festivals). The seven bulls bring the offering to a round conclusion. Total bulls over seven days: 70. The seventh day of Sukkot is called Hoshana Rabbah (Great Hosanna) in later tradition — a day of intense prayer and final sealing of the year's judgments.
33: וּמִנְחָתָם וְנִסְכֵּיהֶם (uminchatam veniskeihem): 'their grain offerings and drink offerings.' Kamishpatam — the final repetition of the proportional formula within the seven-day sequence. By the seventh day, the worshipper has fully internalized the system. The repetition is no longer instruction but confirmation: the system has become the worshipper's liturgical native tongue.
34: וּשְׂעִיר אֶחָד חַטָּאת (use'ir echad chatta't): 'one goat as a sin offering.' The final sin offering of the seven-day Sukkot sequence. Seven consecutive days, seven consecutive sin offerings — one per day, invariant. The seven-fold repetition creates an inescapable theological statement: each day of the most joyous festival in Israel's year requires its own act of atonement. Joy and confession are not opposites in Israel's worship; they are companions.
35: בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁמִינִי עֲצֶרֶת (bayom hashmini atzeret, H6116): 'on the eighth day: a closing assembly.' Atzeret (H6116) — from atzar (H6113, to restrain, hold back, retain, shut in). The atzeret is a gathering that holds the community in place one final day after the festival ends: the LORD detains his people for one more day in his presence. It is called Shemini Atzeret in later tradition. The eighth day is numerologically significant: beyond the week (7+1), beginning a new cycle, associated with covenant renewal and eschatological newness.
36: אِשֶּׁה עֹלָה לְרֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ (isheh olah lere'ach nichoach): 'a fire offering, a burnt offering as a pleasing aroma.' The eighth day offering drops dramatically: one bull, one ram, seven lambs — a return to the minimal festival scale after the week's excess. After 70 bulls over seven days, the eighth day offers one. The contrast is intentional: the week of abundance ends; the atzeret is intimate. It is as though YHWH says: all the feast's grandeur has been offered — now hold back one more day with a simpler gift, just to be here.
37: מִנְחָתָם וְנִסְכֵּיהֶם (minchatam veniskeihem): 'their grain offerings and drink offerings.' Kamishpatam — the final appearance of the proportional formula in the chapter. The eighth day grain and drink offerings follow the same proportions as all other festival offerings. Despite the reduction in animal scale, the quality requirements (temimim, without blemish) and the proportional grammar of the minhah remain unchanged. Intimacy does not diminish precision in Israel's worship.
38: וּשְׂעִיר אֶחָד חַטָּאת (use'ir echad chatta't): 'one male goat as a sin offering.' The eighth day also has its sin offering — the final sin offering in the entire seventh-month festival sequence. The atzeret is not exempt from the acknowledgment of sin even in its final intimacy. Atonement is required as the people depart YHWH's festival presence, just as it was required upon entering it. The festival calendar begins and ends with the need for expiation.
39: אֵלֶּה תַּעֲשׂוּ לַיהוָה בְּמוֹעֲדֵיכֶם (eleh ta'asu la-YHWH bemoe'deikhem, H4150): 'these you shall do for YHWH at your appointed festivals.' The closing summary of the entire festival calendar legislation (Numbers 28-29). Mo'adim (H4150, appointed times) — the festivals are not Israel's creation but YHWH's designated appointments: times he has set for covenant encounter. Milevad nidreikhem u-nidvoteikhem — 'in addition to your vow offerings and freewill offerings' — the mandatory calendar offerings are a floor, not a ceiling; personal votive and freewill offerings are not prohibited but additional.
40: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (vayomer moshe el-b'nei yisrael): 'Moses told the Israelites.' The closing transmission formula. Moses communicates to Israel everything YHWH commanded him — the standard chain of divine speech → Mosaic transmission → Israel's reception. The legislation of Numbers 28-29 (the complete festival sacrifice calendar) is thus passed from YHWH through Moses to the entire nation. The calendar is not priestly arcana but communal law: every Israelite is to know what YHWH requires at his appointed times.
